title: Inference Speed & Model Compression
concept: Running segmentation models on edge devices requires model compression techniques like pruning, quantization, and lightweight backbones.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a critical rule when applying geometric augmentations in segmentation?

options.text: You must apply the exact same transformation to both the image and the mask
You must only augment the background pixels
You must downsample the mask to 1x1 before transforming

exercises.explain: Correct! Image and mask must stay aligned, so they must receive the identical geometric transforms.

cards.title: Fitting a giant in a smartwatch
cards.body: A high-quality U-Net with a ResNet backbone can be 100 megabytes in size and take 200 milliseconds to run on a powerful GPU. But what if you need to run it on a smartphone to segment faces in real-time at 30 FPS, or on a smart glass device? It will lag and drain the battery. How do we compress the model without losing the mask quality?

cards.title: Model Compression
cards.body:
To run segmentation on edge hardware, we use:
1. **Lightweight Backbones** (MobileNet, ShuffleNet).
2. **Pruning** (deleting inactive neurons).
3. **Quantization** (converting weights from 32-bit float to 8-bit integer, reducing size by 75% and speeding up inference).

cards.title: Packing a suitcase
cards.body: Running a raw model on a phone is like trying to fit a fully assembled bike into a suitcase. Compression is like taking the bike apart, folding the frame (pruning/quantization), and packing it efficiently. It takes up a fraction of the space but still functions when unpacked.

cards.title: FP32 to INT8 conversion
cards.body: A U-Net model is 120MB. By applying INT8 quantization, we convert the weight values to 8-bit integers. The model size shrinks to 30MB, and it runs 4x faster on a mobile CPU with almost no visible loss in mask accuracy.

cards.title: Quantization is not lossless
cards.body: You might think quantization has no downside. In reality, converting floats to integers rounds off values. While general shapes are preserved, extreme quantization can cause fine, single-pixel boundaries to blur or break apart, which is risky for medical scans.

left.text:
Size: 120MB, Speed: Slow (5 FPS on phone)
Highest boundary precision
Consumes high power

right.text:
Size: 30MB, Speed: Fast (30 FPS on phone)
Minor loss in fine borders
Low power consumption

exercises.prompt: What is quantization in model compression?

options.text: Converting weights from 32-bit floating points to 8-bit integers
Deleting the decoder branch of the network
Increasing the number of channels in the encoder
Averaging the predictions of multiple networks

exercises.explain: Correct! Quantization reduces memory size and speeds up integer math on edge chips.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Pruning speeds up models by removing weak or inactive weights from the network.
exercises.explain: True. Pruning trims out unimportant connections, making the model sparser and faster.

exercises.sentence: Quantizing a model from FP32 to INT8 typically reduces its file size by ___ percent.
exercises.explain: Since it goes from 32 bits to 8 bits (1/4th the size), the file size drops by 75%.

exercises.prompt: What is a potential downside of extreme quantization in segmentation?

options.text: Very fine boundaries and micro-structures might become blurry or lose precision
The model will automatically convert to an object detector
The learning rate will increase to infinity

exercises.explain: Rounding errors from quantization can degrade the precision of fine boundaries.

exercises.prompt: You are deploying a face-segmentation model for a mobile video chat app. It must run on old phones at 30 FPS. Which optimization strategy is best?

options.text: Use a MobileNet backbone, apply pruning, and quantize the model to INT8 format.
Use a ResNet-101 backbone, disable skip connections, and keep weights in FP32 format.
Run a CRF post-processor on the CPU after every frame.
Remove the decoder and predict masks using a single dense layer.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Swapping to MobileNet, pruning, and INT8 quantization is the industry standard recipe for running real-time segmentation on mobile hardware. Next: diagnosing boundary bleeding!
